planting.note: Plant transplants in fall (September–October in zone 9) for fall and winter bloom, or plant in late winter (January–February) for spring bloom. Osteospermum (African daisy) closes its flowers at night and on cloudy days — the flowers are diurnal. The Serenity series was developed for improved heat tolerance compared to older osteospermum series, allowing some flowering even in mild zone 9 summers. Plants may be cut back by one-third in June to encourage more compact growth and fall rebloom. The deep purple-violet flowers with distinctive blue-black centers produce a rich, jewel-toned color that provides excellent contrast with yellow, orange, and white companions in the cool-season garden.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately moist soil — Osteospermum requires full sun for maximum flowering; the flowers close in shade and on overcast days; Serenity Purple Osteospermum is a compact, heat-tolerant cultivar of African Daisy (Osteospermum ecklonis) producing vivid purple-violet to deep mauve-purple, single daisy flowers with distinctive blue-black central discs that create a striking two-tone color effect between the saturated petals and the nearly black center. The Serenity series represents one of the most heat-tolerant and widely grown osteospermum breeding lines — bred to extend the practical bloom season of this South African native from the strictly cool-only performance of older series into warm-season conditions where mild summers allow continued flowering. Osteospermum provides the richest saturated cool-tone daisy colors available from cool-season annual plants — the purple, pink, and white color range of osteospermum varieties providing the most vivid and large-scale cool-color cool-season flower display available in zone 9's fall-through-spring annual garden. The combination of large flower size (2–3 inches), vivid color, compact mounding form, and the unusual blue-black center disc that creates a subtle bicolor contrast makes Serenity Purple one of the most visually distinctive cool-season annual offerings available for zone 9 fall and winter containers and garden beds.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Osteospermum for Florida Cool-Season Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — African Daisy for Gulf South Fall and Winter Color
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Osteospermum Series for Zone 9

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall (October–November in zone 9); in zone 9 ornamental alliums are not reliably perennial — the bulbs require cool winter temperatures for proper flower induction that zone 9 winters may not reliably provide. Plant fresh bulbs annually for best results, or provide 8–10 weeks of pre-chilling in the refrigerator before planting. Allium 'Gladiator' produces large (4-inch), round, violet-purple flower heads on tall (3–4 foot) stems in April–May in zone 9. The flowers are self-supporting and do not require staking. All Allium foliage has the characteristic onion-garlic scent when crushed. After flowering, the seed heads remain ornamental through summer, providing structural interest even after the flowers have dried.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Allium requires excellent drainage and is susceptible to bulb rot in wet, clay soils; Gladiator is one of the largest and most impressive ornamental Allium hybrids, producing large (4-inch), perfectly spherical, tightly packed flower heads composed of hundreds of small, star-shaped, violet-purple florets on tall (3–4 foot), self-supporting stems — creating one of the most dramatic and architecturally bold spring bulb flower forms available in ornamental gardening. The large, globe-shaped flower heads of Allium 'Gladiator' provide a completely different visual character than the low-growing, daisy-form or trumpet-form flowers that dominate the spring bulb season — the large, floating balls of violet-purple held well above the surrounding plants creating a structural, architectural element that works both in the mixed spring border and as a bold vertical accent in the landscape. The dried seed heads remaining after the flowers fade are fully as attractive as the fresh flowers — the structural, cage-like seed heads maintaining visual interest through summer when most spring bulbs have gone dormant. As a relative of edible onion and garlic, Allium foliage and flowers are completely deer-resistant — an important practical attribute for areas where deer browse regularly.
sources: Netherlands Flower Bulb Information Center — Allium 'Gladiator' Bulb Guide
University of Georgia Extension — Ornamental Allium for Southern Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Spring Bulbs for Gulf South Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 9) or start indoors in winter for spring transplanting. Seeds benefit from cold stratification — sowing in fall allows natural stratification. McKana Giant is an All-America Selections winning columbine series producing large (3–4 inch) flowers in an exceptionally wide color range including blue-and-white, red-and-yellow, pink-and-white, and solid yellow, white, and lavender. In zone 9 columbine is a short-lived perennial (1–3 years) rather than a long-lived perennial — it performs best in partial shade with cool, moist conditions and declines in prolonged summer heat. Columbine self-seeds readily to maintain a garden population. The long, nectar-filled spurs are specifically adapted for hummingbird pollination.

directions: Plant in partial shade — morning sun with afternoon shade or filtered light under deciduous trees; full sun in zone 9 causes premature decline and poor performance in summer heat — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist, humus-rich soil; McKana Giant Columbine is an All-America Selections award-winning hybrid Columbine series producing the characteristic nodding, long-spurred columbine flowers at an unusually large scale (3–4 inch flowers) in a diverse mix of colors and bicolor combinations — including blue-and-white, red-and-yellow, pink-and-white, and various pastels — that displays the elegant, complex, uniquely structured form of Aquilegia flowers at its most dramatic. The five petals, five petal-like sepals, and five nectar-producing spurs (one per petal) of Aquilegia flowers combine to create one of the most structurally intricate and beautiful flower forms available from any commonly grown garden perennial — the long, curved spurs extending behind the flower in a cluster that gives the whole bloom a distinctly exotic, airborne quality. Columbines are outstanding hummingbird plants — the long, tubular, nectar-filled spurs are perfectly adapted for pollination by Ruby-throated Hummingbirds and other long-billed hummingbirds that feed at the spurs while hovering. In zone 9's partial-shade conditions, McKana Giant Columbines perform as reliable winter-to-spring perennials — the attractive, blue-green, three-lobed foliage providing a lacy texture in the shade garden from fall through the March–May bloom period.
sources: All-America Selections — Aquilegia McKana Giant Award
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Columbine for Gulf South Partial Shade
University of Georgia Extension — Aquilegia Species and Hybrids for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Start seeds indoors in late summer (July–August in zone 9) for fall transplanting; in zone 9 foxglove is grown as a winter annual or biennial — plants established in fall bloom in March–May of the following spring. Camelot Cream is part of the Camelot series, an All-America Selections winning foxglove strain bred for first-year flowering from seed (eliminating the traditional 2-year biennial cycle) and superior heat tolerance compared to older foxglove strains. The cream-white flowers with burgundy-red spotting inside the tubular bells provide a classic foxglove color combination. All parts of Digitalis purpurea are toxic — wear gloves when handling and plant away from areas where children or pets might contact the foliage. Self-seeds reliably in zone 9 gardens with appropriate shade and moisture.

directions: Plant in partial shade — filtered light under tall pines or deciduous trees provides ideal conditions in zone 9; full shade reduces flowering while full sun in zone 9 causes stress and premature decline — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic, humus-rich, consistently moist soil; Camelot Cream Foxglove is an All-America Selections award-winning cultivar producing tall (4–5 foot) spikes densely packed with tubular, pendant, cream-white flowers marked with purple-red spots inside the bell — the classic foxglove color pattern displayed in a large-flowered, first-year-blooming strain that provides one of the most dramatic vertical accents available from any cool-season annual or biennial in partial shade conditions. The tall flowering spikes of foxglove provide a vertical element that is rare among shade-garden flowering plants — most shade-tolerant species producing horizontal or mounding floral displays, while foxglove's 4–5 foot tall spikes create strong vertical lines and tower above companion plants in the partial shade border. The tubular pendant flowers are specifically adapted for bumblebee pollination — the large bees entering the tubular bell to access nectar positioned at the base, brushing against stamens as they enter and exit and providing efficient cross-pollination. In zone 9 Camelot Cream foxgloves grown from late-summer seed provide an extraordinary spring shade garden display in March–May that few other partial-shade plants can approach for scale and visual drama.
sources: All-America Selections — Digitalis Camelot Cream Award
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Foxglove for Gulf South Partial Shade Gardens
University of Georgia Extension — Digitalis purpurea as a Southern Biennial

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (October–November in zone 9) for winter establishment and spring bloom; Clarkia amoena does not transplant well and must be direct-sown. Cool soil (50–65°F) is required for good germination. In zone 9 fall sowing is the appropriate approach — spring sowing in warming soil produces poor germination and short-lived, heat-stressed plants. Grace Pink produces large (2–3 inch), semi-double, satiny pink flowers with a slightly darker pink center zone — a classic, clear, pure pink that is effective in mixed cool-season plantings. Plants in zone 9 bloom February–April before summer heat arrives. Self-seeds modestly in zone 9 where soil conditions are appropriate. Godetia (Clarkia amoena) is native to the Pacific Coast of North America.

directions: Direct sow in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with consistent cool-season moisture — Godetia germinates and grows best in cool conditions (50–65°F) and declines rapidly as summer heat arrives; Grace Pink Godetia is a compact selection of Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia amoena), a Pacific Coast native wildflower producing satiny, semi-double, clear rose-pink flowers (2–3 inches) on upright stems in late winter and spring — one of the most elegantly textured cool-season annuals available, with a silky petal surface that catches and reflects light in a way that gives the flowers an unusual luminous quality. The satiny, slightly translucent texture of godetia petals is entirely different from the matte, paper-like texture of many other cool-season annuals, providing an unusual visual refinement that makes godetia flowers one of the most elegant and graceful cut flowers available from a direct-sow cool-season crop. The clear, pure pink of Grace Pink — without the purple or salmon undertones that characterize many pink annual flowers — works harmoniously with virtually any companion planting in the cool-season garden, from blue violas to white alyssum to yellow snapdragons. In zone 9 fall-sown godetia provides a distinctive and relatively under-used February–April bloom display that is more refined and elegant in character than the more commonly planted cool-season annuals.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Clarkia amoena for California Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Fall-Sown Cool-Season Annuals for Gulf South
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Godetia for Florida Winter Annual Gardens
